text: So this is another in our series of complex or fissureless robotic lobectomies. This is going to be a left lower lobectomy. And it's actually a hilar tumor, which I think adds to the complexity of this case. As I teach people how to do these robotic lobectomies, they seem to struggle with with lobectomies where there is a thick, incomplete fissure. For a lower lobectomy, that generally means that you have to do a bottom to top approach, similar to the D'Amico method for VATS lobectomies, if you don't want to dive through the thick fissure, which tends to lead to air leaks and bleeding. So this is a good demonstration of a left lower lobectomy with a bottom to top technique. set up for robotic left lower lobectomy. And if you've seen the previous videos, it's essentially the same. For lower lobectomies, I use three eights and one twelve. The twelve is the staple port. All the staples are going to be fired from the medial direction on the lower lobectomy. That's different than an upper lobectomy where I put a second twelve. but you don't need that on a lower lobe. My first port is the camera port, and I find that at the sternal notch or the junction between the sternum and the xiphoid. That's my x-axis, and that's at the posterior axillary line, which is my y-axis. There should be ten to twelve centimeters between the camera port and the medial staple port, which is usually one inner space higher. If you count out the ribs, which I don't, but But if you do, this is usually the sixth inner space, and this is the seventh inner space. And then you triangulate the assistant port, which is a twelve millimeter air seal right at the junction of the chest and the diaphragm, as low as you can possibly go. And then you only need six to eight centimeters between the lateral ports, which are arms three and four, which should be in the same inner space as the camera.

The CT scan reveals a tumor located in the left hilum of the lung, adjacent to the inferior pulmonary vein. The surgeon holds a sponge roll over the tumor, which is positioned near the descending aorta. The tumor is carefully separated from the aorta, allowing for some mobility. The surgeon dissects the inferior pulmonary vein, aiming to take it close to the left atrium for tumor margin. Both sides of the vein are dissected, focusing on the cephaloid side. The posterior mediastinal pleura is then dissected, with the anatomy showing the vein as the lowest structure, followed by the left lower lobe bronchus and the interlobar pulmonary artery above it.

If you see the black shaft of the instrument when you're doing delicate dissection of pulmonary artery, pulmonary vein, you're probably not close enough. And you're not taking advantage of what the robot has to offer you. Remember, the disadvantage of the robot is that you don't have haptic feedback. So you have to compensate for that in some way. And I talk about learning how to feel with your eyes. You gotta take advantage of the optics of the camera, the ten times magnification, the stereo vision. If you bring the camera in close, the visual cues of the tissue displacement will allow you to tell how hard or soft something is.

The surgeon performs delicate dissection of the subcarinal lymph node located between the inferior pulmonary vein and the lower lobe bronchus, using close camera proximity to visualize the silver tips of the instruments. The subcarinal lymph node is removed to aid in isolating the inferior pulmonary vein. The surgeon continues with medial hilar dissection, targeting nodes between the left upper and lower lobe bronchus. The superior pulmonary vein becomes visible as the surgeon examines the fissure, searching for the yellow hue of the pulmonary artery. A crossing vein is identified and clipped, revealing the underlying pulmonary artery.

The surgeon uses medium clips to secure small branches of the pulmonary artery and vein, avoiding the use of bipolar cautery to prevent bleeding. The descending aorta is shifted leftward, complicating access to the inferior pulmonary vein. The surgeon employs an endolead catheter to guide the stapler safely behind the pulmonary vein, ensuring precise division without injury. The vein is successfully divided, and dissection proceeds on the bottom side of the left lower lobe bronchus, revealing the basilar and superior segmental bronchi adjacent to the pulmonary artery. A hilar lymph node, located between the left upper and lower lobe bronchi, is identified for removal.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to elevate the lung, exposing the space between the basilar segmental bronchi and the superior segmental bronchi. The dissection focuses on isolating the lower lobe bronchus, with the silver tips of the instruments visible as they separate the suprasegmental bronchus from the suprasegmental artery. The surgeon employs a tip-up instrument to safely traverse the left lower lobe bronchus, preparing to divide it using a green load stapler, while ensuring adequate space with a retrograde pull of the robnil catheter.

The surgeon dissects the space between the suprasegmental artery and the basilar trunk of the pulmonary artery. A small unnamed branch is clipped using two clips on the staying side, followed by division with hot scissors. The superior segmental branch is divided first using a gray load staple from the medial staple port, followed by the basilar trunk. The Rob Nell catheter aids in guiding the stapler through the tight space. The surgeon prepares to divide the fissure, ensuring the bronchial and artery stumps are positioned correctly outside the stapler.

The surgeon uses a blue load stapler to divide the thick fissure of the disproportionately large left lower lobe, ensuring the bronchial and artery stumps are below the staple line. The fourth arm is used to hold the tissue away, facilitating the stapling process. The lobe is then packed into an anchor bag for removal.
